title: Thrown stoneware jug with pulled spout
subtitle: A wheel-thrown jug body with a pinched-and-shaped pouring spout, glaze-fired at cone 6
excerpt: A jug is a thrown cylinder with a belly, a neck, a rim, a handle, and a spout shaped into the soft-wet rim before any other handling. This tutorial covers a 1 kg smooth stoneware jug holding around 700 ml, with a pulled spout shaped before the pot leaves the wheel and a pulled handle attached at leather-hard. Bisque fired at cone 06 and glaze fired at cone 6.
sourceNotes: Thrown jug construction derived from first principles. V&A open-access collection medieval English jug photographs for the historical-form reference. Cassell's The Book of the Home (1880s editions). Orton Ceramic Foundation cone temperature reference.

glossaryTerms.definition: Pushing the clay mound into a state where it rotates without wobbling under the hands.
The drying stage where the clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim, join, and attach handles.
Air-dried but unfired clay. The final stage before bisque firing.
The first kiln firing, around cone 06 (999°C), that converts greenware into porous bisqueware ready for glazing.
A handle drawn out from a wedge of clay by repeatedly stroking with a wet hand until the cross-section tapers and the handle reaches working length.
Water-based wax emulsion brushed onto bisque to mask areas the glaze should not stick to.

projectSchedule.title: Throw the body and shape the spout
projectSchedule.body: Centre 1 kg, throw a cylinder, swell the belly, collar the neck, level the rim, and pull the spout before the rim stiffens.

projectSchedule.title: Trim and attach handle
projectSchedule.body: At leather-hard. Trim the foot ring, then pull a handle from a separate wedge and attach to the body.

projectSchedule.title: Bone-dry check before bisque
projectSchedule.body: Wait for the jug to reach uniform room temperature throughout. The handle attachment area is the slowest to dry.

projectSchedule.title: Bisque fire at cone 06
projectSchedule.body: Load on a kiln-washed shelf, handle clear of neighbouring work. Fire to cone 06 (999°C). Cool inside the kiln.

projectSchedule.title: Wax and glaze
projectSchedule.body: Wax the foot ring. Pour glaze inside first, then dip the exterior holding by the handle.

projectSchedule.title: Glaze fire at cone 6
projectSchedule.body: Load on a kiln-washed shelf. Fire to cone 6 (1222°C). Cool with the lid closed below 80°C before opening.

content.text: A jug carries everything a thrown cylinder teaches plus three additional moves: a belly swelled outward, a neck collared in, and a spout shaped from the soft rim before the pot is cut from the wheel. The handle goes on at leather-hard. This tutorial works in 1 kg of smooth stoneware to produce a jug holding around 700 ml; water-jug scale rather than milk-jug scale. A potter's wheel and access to a kiln firing to cone 6 are both required.

attrs.title: Silica + kiln safety
attrs.body: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard and silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only. Greenware sanding happens outdoors with a mask and never in a kitchen-air-shared space. For the kiln firing: ensure ventilation, either by downdraft vent or by an open window with an extractor. Wear welder's shade-5 glasses when peephole-watching at high temperatures. Never open a hot kiln below 80°C. Keep a Class A fire extinguisher within reach of the studio area.

items.name: Smooth stoneware
items.quantity: 1 kg (800 g body + 200 g for handle)

content.text: Throwing the body
1. Centre 800 g and pull a tall cylinder
Wedge 800 g and slap onto the bat.
Centring

is complete when the mound feels still. Open to a floor 90-100 mm wide, leaving a 6 mm floor thickness. Pull a cylinder in three passes to roughly 150-170 mm tall with walls 5-6 mm thick throughout. This is the starting cylinder; the jug will be shaped from here.
2. Swell the belly

Slow the wheel. Place the pads of the fingers inside the cylinder at the lower third; place the outside hand against the same height on the outside, palm flat. Press outward gently from inside while the outside hand supports without pressing. The wall belly outward 10-15 mm at the widest point. Stop the swell at the lower third; the cylinder above this point stays the original diameter and becomes the neck and shoulder.
3. Collar the neck

Wrap both hands around the upper third of the cylinder, fingers loosely interlocked. Squeeze inward gently while lifting upward; the neck narrows and rises slightly. Aim to reduce the neck diameter by 15-20 mm compared to the body diameter. The narrower neck gives the jug its profile and concentrates the pouring flow at the spout.
4. Level and compress the rim

Trim the rim level with the needle tool. Compress with a damp chamois folded over the edge or with the pad of a fingertip. The rim must be slightly thicker than the wall below it (about 8 mm) because the spout will be pulled out from this rim and a thin rim tears as the spout forms.
Pulling the spout

Stop the wheel. The spout forms while the rim is still soft and the body is freshly thrown; if the rim begins to stiffen before this step is done, the spout will tear out instead of stretching. Spot the position where the spout will sit; directly opposite where the handle will go later. Mark this position on the bat with a fingernail.

Wet the index finger and thumb of one hand and the index finger of the other. Place the wet index finger and thumb on the outside of the rim at the spout position, about 25 mm apart. Place the wet inside finger between them, on the inside of the rim. Now stroke downward and outward with the inside finger while the outside finger and thumb pinch gently together, supporting and shaping the clay as it stretches forward.

Three or four slow strokes pull the rim into a forward-projecting lip about 15-20 mm long. The inside finger creates the channel down which liquid will pour; the outside finger and thumb keep the edges of the spout from spreading flat. The lip should curve gently downward at the front; water needs to leave the spout, not run back along the underside of the rim.
Wire off the base. Lift the bat off the wheel and set aside for the body to firm.
Trimming the foot ring
When the body reaches
leather-hard

(8-24 hours, covered loosely with plastic to slow the rim drying), invert onto the wheel head. Centre by tapping the body gently while spinning. Trim a foot ring 80-90 mm in diameter (about 20 mm inside the body wall) with the loop tool. Bevel both edges of the ring.
Pulling and attaching the handle

Take the remaining 200 g of wedged clay and form into a thick carrot shape about 100 mm long, fat end at the top. Hold the fat end upright in one hand. Wet the other hand and stroke firmly down the length of the clay carrot in long even strokes; the clay stretches and tapers. After eight to twelve strokes the
pulled handle
is around 150 mm long with a tapered cross-section that fits comfortably between thumb and forefinger.

Cut the stretched handle off the parent wedge with a wire. Score and slip the upper attachment point on the body just below the rim, opposite the spout, and press the fat end of the handle on firmly. Curve the handle down and outward to a natural grip position, then score and slip the lower attachment point on the body belly. Press the tapered end on. Smooth the joins with a damp finger.
Allow the joined jug to dry slowly under plastic for the first 24 hours; the handle attachment is the most likely place to crack because it dries faster than the body. Then uncover and let dry to
bone-dry
over four to seven days.
Bisque firing
Load with the handle clear of any neighbouring work; the slight expansion during firing can crack a handle pressed against a wall.
Bisque fire
to cone 06 (999°C) with a slow climb through the early hours. Cool inside the kiln to room temperature before unloading.
Glazing
Wipe the bisque jug with a damp sponge to remove kiln dust. Apply
wax resist

to the foot ring. The interior of a jug needs glaze for water-tightness; pour glaze inside first. Hold the jug at a 30-degree tilt, pour in about 150-200 ml of stirred glaze, rotate the jug fully so the glaze coats the entire interior surface, then pour the excess back into the bucket through the spout. Pour smoothly to leave an even coat without a thick line at the spout edge.

For the exterior, hold the jug by the handle and dip the body into the glaze bucket. Submerge to within 3-4 mm of the foot ring; rotate as the jug is lifted clear. The handle will get a half-dip from holding; touch up the unglazed surfaces with a loaded liner brush. Drain and set to dry.
Glaze firing

Load the jug on a freshly kiln-washed shelf, handle and spout clear of any neighbour. Fire to cone 6 (1222°C) following the kiln manufacturer's cone 6 schedule. Cool with the lid closed until the temperature reads 80°C or below before opening.
What it looks like when it is right

A working jug pours without dribbling along the underside of the spout. Test before glazing: fill the bisque jug with water and pour. If the water runs back along the spout, the spout angle was not curved sharply enough downward at the tip; correct on the next throw. After glazing, the interior is fully sealed and the exterior glaze is even with no bare patches at the handle attachments.
Variations

A milk jug at 500 g of clay holds around 350 ml and uses the same sequence at smaller scale. A taller pitcher at 1.5 kg holds around 1.2 litres and needs a stouter handle, which means starting with 300 g of handle clay rather than 200 g. Speckled stoneware throws and glazes the same way and produces a more rustic finished surface.

items.symptom: Jug dribbles along the underside of the spout when poured
items.cause: Spout tip not curved sharply enough downward, or spout channel too wide
items.fix: Test pour from bisque before glazing; correct the spout angle on the next throw by stroking the inside finger more steeply downward at the end of the spout pull

items.symptom: Handle cracks at the attachment point during drying
items.cause: Handle attached when the body was too dry, or attachment dried faster than the body
items.fix: Attach handles when both body and handle clay are at the same leather-hard stage; cover the joined jug with plastic for the first 24 hours to slow drying

items.symptom: Spout tears as it is pulled
items.cause: Rim too thin, or the rim had already begun to stiffen before the spout was shaped
items.fix: Leave the rim 8 mm thick at the spout-pulling step; shape the spout immediately after compressing the rim while the clay is still freshly thrown

items.symptom: Glaze pools at the bottom of the inside and runs in the firing
items.cause: Too much glaze poured in, or interior not rotated enough to drain fully
items.fix: Pour smaller amounts of glaze (150 ml for a 700 ml jug); rotate fully and tip out all unabsorbed glaze through the spout before setting the jug upright
